features.summary: AMD reported record Q2 2022 results with revenue of $6.6 billion, up 70% year-over-year, driven by strong performance across all segments including the first full quarter of Xilinx integration. Data Center revenue grew 83% to $1.5 billion led by EPYC processor demand, while Client revenue increased 25% to $2.2 billion on record mobile processor sales. Gaming revenue rose 32% to $1.7 billion with semi-custom growth offsetting graphics declines, and Embedded revenue reached $1.3 billion primarily from Xilinx products. Gross margin expanded to 54% with record operating and net income both more than doubling from the prior year.
features.management_outlook: Management maintained full-year 2022 revenue guidance of approximately $26.3 billion despite taking a more conservative PC market outlook, now expecting mid-teens decline versus previous high single-digit expectations. Q3 2022 revenue is expected at approximately $6.7 billion, with growth led by Data Center and Embedded segments. The company anticipates strong second-half performance driven by new 5-nanometer product ramps including Ryzen 7000 desktop processors, Genoa server CPUs, and RDNA 3 graphics cards launching in Q4. Management expressed confidence in continued Data Center growth into 2023 with strong customer visibility and demand for next-generation products.
features.risks: The company faces headwinds from a weakening PC market with management now expecting mid-teens decline versus earlier projections, and gaming graphics demand has softened due to macro conditions impacting discretionary spending. Supply constraints persist in certain areas including Embedded/Xilinx products and some server components, though additional capacity is expected to come online. There are mixed enterprise trends with some deals taking longer to close due to macro backdrop, and potential inventory buildup in PC channels requires rebalancing in the second half.
features.key_drivers: Strong data center momentum continues with 83% year-over-year growth driven by cloud instance ramps and enterprise OEM adoption of EPYC processors, supported by upcoming Genoa launch with strong customer pull. The Xilinx integration is performing well with approximately 20% sequential growth on a pro forma basis and significant supply chain improvements. New product cycles provide growth catalysts with multiple 5-nanometer products ramping in Q4 including client, server, and graphics offerings. Semi-custom console business remains robust and is expected to peak in Q3, while the company benefits from expanded manufacturing capacity and supply coming online to meet demand.
